title: --userns=keep-id causes OCI permission denied on multi-user system
body:
### Issue Description

Hi,

this is essentially a manifestation of https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/16350 but unfortunately in an environment where we can't really use the workarounds mentioned there.

We're trying to use distrobox, which uses `--userns=keep-id` behind the scenes.

A minimal podman reproduction looks like this:
```
podman run --rm -it --userns=keep-id alpine:latest
Error: crun: open `/home/nq5949/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/a24a12efe69c3098002800be167189aaf71ad35c4b65c87fb3e6a4342725faca/merged`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

Maybe some background about our setup:
* We use AD login with manually created / synchronized subuid and subgid files for AD users. This part is working fine.
* We're using NFS for /home. We know that NFS is not supported for podman storage, so we mount `/home/$USER/.local/share/containers` to a real filesystem. This is also working.

The OCI permission denied error can be worked-around as mentioned in #16350:
```
chmod 701 /home/$USER
```

However, in a multi-user system this seems to be insecure.

Even worse: in our shared PC pool, we mount home folders with kerberized NFS, for security reasons. In this case users without a kerberos ticket can never access home folders. So here, the workaround is not working at all.

Any idea how we could solve this? Maybe moving the graphroot somewhere else could work? But giving `x` permission to all users still seems not desirable....

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `chmod 700 /home/$USER`
2. `podman run --rm -it --userns=keep-id alpine:latest`


### Describe the results you received

```
`/home/nq5949/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/a24a12efe69c3098002800be167189aaf71ad35c4b65c87fb3e6a4342725faca/merged`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

### Describe the results you expected

Starting the container

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
> Any idea how we could solve this? Maybe moving the graphroot somewhere else could work? But giving x permission to all users still seems not desirable....

Moving the graphroot seems to work:
`~/.config/containers/storage.conf`:
```
[storage]
driver = "overlay"
rootless_storage_path = "/podman/nq5949/storage"
```

With these changes now at least the permissions on /home/$USER don't matter anymore.

But what are the security implications giving other users `x` access to the graphroot folder? Will other users be able to access any relevant information in the graphroot? Or do the files there have proper permissions to prevent issues?

Moving the graphroot off of NFS is definitely the most sane thing you can do in this configuration; rootless containers + NFS is not a combination that works well together.

Our permissions should be sufficient to prevent other users from accessing anything concerning from another user's graphroot. We need the a+x to let the UIDs and GIDs from subuid/subgid access the container, and permissions are set appropriately for them to access the relevant layers, but nothing else should be accessible.

@giuseppe Do we need to use the new mount API code path for all idmapped containers not just one the main uid is not mapped, ref https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/49eb5af3013ef64bd017a1fd545c802e3eb230a1?

https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/49eb5af3013ef64bd017a1fd545c802e3eb230a1 works when the underlying file system honors CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE.

Network file systems can't be used normally, the fact they don't honor CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE means the additional privileges in the user namespace are pointless.  The only way to get it working is to use `fuse-overlayfs` that remaps IDs at runtime.

Unless I am missing something, this is the usual "podman on NFS" problem and there is nothing we can do to get it working with native overlay.

> Moving the graphroot off of NFS is definitely the most sane thing you can do in this configuration; rootless containers + NFS is not a combination that works well together.

Ok, then we'll do that. (It is not on NFS, it's a mountpoint with a parent directory that's on NFS) We'll have to see how we can automate that for many users. Probably we need to ship the storage.conf file as part of the home skeleton and somehow auto-generate it, so that we can have different paths for different users.

> Unless I am missing something, this is the usual "podman on NFS" problem and there is nothing we can do to get it working with native overlay.

Not really, because the graphroot, `/home/$USER/.local/share/containers` is the mountpoint of an ext4 filesystem. 
So this problem is more like: "If some parent directory of the graphRoot is on NFS (the graphRoot itself is not) and that NFS mount uses kerberos, --userns=keep-id is broken".

Not sure if it changes much about this discussion or the solution though :-)
